question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted environment variable prefixed with 'HEDERA_' that maps to a fee-related hiero property (e.g., HEDERA_MIRROR_IMPORTER_PARSER_RECORD_ENTITY_PERSIST_TOKENS) so that convertEnvironmentVariable() silently migrates it to a 'HIERO_' equivalent, overriding the intended fee persistence configuration and causing transaction fees to be stored or processed outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user who controls any externally-readable property source (e.g., via a config file on a shared filesystem) inject a 'hedera.mirror' prefixed property targeting a fee schedule key, causing migrate() to silently produce a higher-priority 'hiero.mirror' property that overrides the legitimate fee schedule configuration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: convertEnvironmentVariable()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the case-insensitive check in Strings.CI.startsWith(name, 'hedera') to inject a mixed-case environment variable like 'hEdErA_MIRROR_FEE_SCHEDULE_VALUE' that passes the hedera-prefix filter, gets migrated to a 'hiero' fee property, and overrides the intended transaction fee limits?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: convertPropertyName()] Can an unprivileged user craft a property name like 'hedera.mirror.importer.parser.record.entity.fee.amount' that survives the Strings.CI.replace(name, 'hedera.', 'hiero.', 1) transformation and is inserted as a higher-priority hiero property, causing the fee amount to be set to an attacker-controlled value outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user who can write to any EnumerablePropertySource (e.g., application.properties on a writable mount) inject a 'hedera.*' fee property so that onApplicationEvent() iterates it, calls migrate(), and inserts a migrated hiero fee property via propertySources.addAfter(), effectively bypassing the intended fee configuration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that migrate() performs no value validation before inserting properties into the LinkedHashMap, allowing them to inject an arbitrarily large or negative fee amount via a 'hedera.*' property that is then migrated to a 'hiero.*' fee property, causing transaction fees to be set outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' property with a value of '0' or '-1' for a fee-related configuration key, causing migrate() to propagate this zero/negative fee value into the hiero property namespace with higher priority, resulting in transaction fees being set to zero or negative values outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the LOWEST_PRECEDENCE ordering of getOrder() to ensure that the migrator runs after all other listeners, so that a 'hedera.*' fee property injected late in the startup sequence is still migrated and inserted as a higher-priority source, overriding the intended fee configuration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' property whose name, after Strings.CI.replace transformation, collides with an existing 'hiero.*' fee property, causing the migrated MapPropertySource inserted via addAfter() to shadow the legitimate fee configuration with an attacker-controlled value?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: convertEnvironmentVariable()] Can an unprivileged user inject an environment variable 'HEDERA_MIRROR_IMPORTER_PARSER_RECORD_ENTITY_PERSIST_FEES=false' that convertEnvironmentVariable() transforms to 'HIERO_MIRROR_IMPORTER_PARSER_RECORD_ENTITY_PERSIST_FEES=false', disabling fee persistence and causing transaction fees to be silently dropped outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that migrate() uses propertySources.addAfter() (not addFirst()) to insert the migrated source, and by controlling which property source the hedera property appears in, manipulate the effective priority of the migrated fee property to override the intended fee schedule?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user who can set JVM system properties (e.g., via -D flags in a shared environment) inject 'hedera.mirror.fee.maxAmount=999999999' as a system property, causing onApplicationEvent() to enumerate it from the system property source and migrate it to a hiero fee property that overrides the intended maximum fee amount?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user craft a property name containing 'hedera.' multiple times (e.g., 'hedera.hedera.mirror.fee.amount') so that Strings.CI.replace(name, 'hedera.', 'hiero.', 1) only replaces the first occurrence, producing 'hiero.hedera.mirror.fee.amount', which may match an unexpected fee configuration key and modify transaction fees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: convertPropertyName()] Can an unprivileged user inject a property 'hedera.mirror.importer.parser.record.entity.persist.tokens=false' that convertPropertyName() migrates to 'hiero.mirror.importer.parser.record.entity.persist.tokens=false', disabling token (and thus fee schedule) persistence and causing custom fees to be ignored outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of any allowlist or denylist in migrate() to inject any arbitrary 'hedera.*' property name targeting fee-related configuration, with no restriction on which property keys are migrated, allowing unrestricted fee parameter modification?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that onApplicationEvent() iterates ALL EnumerablePropertySource instances without filtering by source type or origin, so that a malicious property source added to the environment (e.g., via a Spring extension point) containing 'hedera.*' fee properties is also migrated and inserted with elevated priority?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' property with a specially crafted value (e.g., a SpEL expression or property placeholder like '${some.other.fee.property}') that, when migrated and inserted as a hiero property, is later resolved by Spring to an attacker-controlled fee value, modifying transaction fees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' fee property with a value referencing another property placeholder (e.g., 'hedera.mirror.fee.max=${ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_VAR}') that migrate() copies verbatim into the hiero namespace, and which Spring later resolves to an attacker-controlled value, bypassing fee design constraints?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the migrated property source is inserted via addAfter() (giving it lower priority than the original source but higher than sources below it) to create a fee property override that takes effect only when the legitimate hiero fee property is absent, silently setting fees to an attacker-controlled value?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' property targeting a fee-related boolean flag (e.g., 'hedera.mirror.importer.parser.record.entity.persist.assessedCustomFees=false') that migrate() propagates to the hiero namespace, disabling assessed custom fee persistence and causing fee accounting to deviate from design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: convertEnvironmentVariable()] Can an unprivileged user inject an environment variable with a 'HEDERA_' prefix that maps to a fee schedule update interval or fee schedule file property, causing convertEnvironmentVariable() to migrate it to the hiero namespace and override the fee schedule refresh rate, allowing stale or attacker-influenced fee schedules to persist?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the log.warn() call in migrate() that logs all migrated property keys to extract sensitive fee configuration key names from application logs, enabling reconnaissance for further fee manipulation attacks?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a large number of 'hedera.*' fee-related properties to cause migrate() to build a very large LinkedHashMap, leading to excessive memory consumption during application startup and a denial-of-service that prevents the correct fee configuration from being loaded?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that onApplicationEvent() does not check whether a migrated hiero property already exists before calling addAfter(), allowing a 'hedera.*' fee property to silently override an explicitly set 'hiero.*' fee property in a lower-priority source, modifying transaction fees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged user inject a 'hedera.*' property with an empty string value for a fee-related configuration key, causing migrate() to propagate the empty value to the hiero namespace, which may be interpreted as zero or null by the fee processing logic, resulting in fees being set to zero outside design parameters?
